section.title: Hosea Redeems Gomer
section.sectionRationale: The shortest chapter is the emotional center of the marriage narrative. God tells Hosea to go and love his adulterous wife again and to buy her back at a price. This costly redemption of an unworthy spouse becomes the clearest picture in the book of the redeeming love of God, and it closes with a promise of Israel seeking the LORD and David their king.
section.thesis: Hosea is commanded to love and redeem his unfaithful wife at real cost, enacting the LORD's love for wayward Israel, and pointing to a future day when a chastened people will return to seek the LORD and their Messianic king.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the command to love her again; the purchase and terms of restoration; and the promise of Israel's return to the LORD and David their king in the last days.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to me, “Go show {{aheb|love}} to your wife again, though she is loved by another and is an adulteress. Love her as the LORD loves the Israelites, though they turn to other gods and love to offer raisin cakes to idols.”
So I {{karah|bought}} her for fifteen shekels of silver and a homer and a lethech of barley.
Then I said to her, “You must live with me for many days; you must not be promiscuous or belong to another, and I will do the same for you.”

units.label: The promise of return
units.note: seeking the LORD and David their king

verses.text: For the Israelites must live many days without king or prince, without sacrifice or sacred pillar, and without ephod or idol.
Afterward, the people of Israel will {{shuv|return}} and seek the LORD their God and David their king. They will come trembling to the LORD and to His goodness in the last days.

aheb.gloss: To love, with warmth and choice. It is used both of Hosea's command to love his straying wife and of the LORD's love for wayward Israel. The same verb even describes Israel loving raisin cakes offered to idols, sharpening the contrast between misplaced human love and God's steadfast, redeeming love.

karah.gloss: To acquire or purchase, here to buy back. Hosea pays a price in silver and barley to reclaim his own wife, who has become another's. The costly buying back pictures redemption, the LORD paying to reclaim a people who had sold themselves into unfaithfulness.

shuv.gloss: To turn, to return, to repent. It is one of Hosea's key words for genuine repentance, a turning back to the LORD. The promise that Israel will return and seek the LORD their God is the hope toward which the whole book strains, fulfilled in true repentance and faith.

basic.context: God tells Hosea to go after his wife again, even though she has been unfaithful and now belongs to another. Hosea has to buy her back, paying silver and barley to reclaim the woman who was already his. Then he sets terms: she is to live faithfully with him. God explains the sign: Israel will go a long time without king, sacrifice, or idol, and then finally turn back to seek the LORD and the coming king in David's line.
basic.hermeneutics: This tiny chapter is deliberately about redemption at a price. The command to love again is stunning precisely because the wife does not deserve it, and that is the point: it mirrors God's love for undeserving Israel. Verse 5 lifts the eyes to the future, a repentant people seeking the LORD and David their king, language the Bible fills out in the Messiah.
basic.originalLanguages: The verb love, aheb, is used four times in verse 1, tying Hosea's love, God's love, and even Israel's misplaced love for idols into one pointed contrast. Hosea buys, karah, his wife back, a picture of redemption. And the promised turning point is shuv, to return or repent, Hosea's great word for coming home to God.
basic.typology: Hosea buying back his adulterous wife foreshadows Christ redeeming His unfaithful people at the cost of His own blood. David their king in verse 5 points beyond David to the promised Son of David, the Messiah, under whom the people will finally return.
basic.christ: The costly purchase of an unworthy bride is fulfilled at the cross, where Christ redeems the church He loves. The promise that Israel will seek David their king in the last days is fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David, to whom repentant people come trembling and find His goodness. He is both the price and the King.
basic.god: We learn that God's love is not a reward for the lovely but a costly commitment to the unfaithful. He does not merely feel love; He pays to reclaim His own. And His discipline, the long years without king or sacrifice, is aimed at producing a genuine, trembling return.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 6:20 says we were bought at a price. Ezekiel 34 and 37 promise one shepherd, David, over God's people. Romans 11 speaks of Israel's future turning to the Lord.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 3 is the marriage narrative's beating heart: love that redeems the undeserving at a price, and a people who will one day return to the LORD and their Davidic king. It points directly to Christ, who buys back His bride and reigns as the Son of David forever.

context.literary: A brief, first-person narrative that completes the marriage sign of chapters 1 and 2. Its five verses mirror the earlier arc in miniature: love, redemption, a period of waiting and deprivation, and a promise of restoration. Verse 5 forms a bridge into the oracles of chapters 4 to 14.
context.historical: The purchase price, fifteen shekels of silver plus a homer and a lethech of barley, roughly equals the price of a slave, underscoring Gomer's degraded condition and the cost of reclaiming her. The list in verse 4, king, prince, sacrifice, sacred pillar, ephod, and idol, describes the coming exile when both legitimate worship and idolatrous props would be stripped away.
context.canonical: The chapter joins the Bible's redemption theme, being bought back at a price, to its royal hope, the return to David their king, both of which converge in Jesus the Redeemer and Son of David.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To display, through Hosea's costly love for his fallen wife, the redeeming love of the LORD for unfaithful Israel, and to promise a future genuine return to God under a Davidic king.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The command to Hosea is a specific prophetic sign, not a general rule about remarriage. The abiding truths are the costliness of redemption, the freeness of God's love toward the undeserving, and the certainty of a repentant future for His people.

debates.question: Who is the woman in verse 1, Gomer or another woman?

views.label: Gomer restored
views.case: The phrase love your wife again most naturally means the same wife from chapter 1, Gomer, now fallen into another man's possession and needing to be bought back.

views.label: A fresh sign with a woman
views.case: A minority read it as a new symbolic act, but the continuity of the marriage story and the word again favor the restoration of Gomer.

debates.bearing: The redemptive meaning is the same either way: the LORD reclaims a people who have given themselves to others, at real cost, out of sheer love.

debates.question: Who is David their king in verse 5?

views.label: The Messianic Son of David
views.case: David had long been dead, so the prophecy looks to a future ruler in David's line, the Messiah, under whom God's people are reunited.

views.label: A restored Davidic monarchy
views.case: It first signals the reunion of the northern kingdom with the Davidic dynasty in Judah, a hope the New Testament shows fulfilled and surpassed in Jesus.

debates.bearing: Both readings terminate in Christ, the Son of David, to whom the repentant people finally come trembling to His goodness.

study.originalLanguages: Verse 1 hammers the word love, aheb, four times: Hosea is to love his wife, as the LORD loves Israel, though they love other gods and love raisin cakes offered to idols. The contrast is devastating and beautiful, human love scattered on idols, divine love fixed on the unworthy. Hosea then buys, karah, his wife back for the price of a slave, a living picture of redemption. And the horizon is shuv, the return, Hosea's word for true repentance, when Israel will turn back to seek the LORD their God and David their king.

typology.type: Hosea buying back his adulterous wife at a price
typology.fulfillment: Christ redeeming the church, His unfaithful bride, at the cost of His own blood, 1 Corinthians 6:20, Ephesians 5:25-27.

typology.type: David their king, sought in the last days
typology.fulfillment: Jesus the Son of David, the Messiah under whom God's people are gathered and to whom they come in repentance.

typology.type: The many days without king, sacrifice, or idol
typology.fulfillment: A season of stripping and waiting that ends in a true return, answered in the gospel's call to repentance and faith.

study.christ: This chapter is one of the Old Testament's clearest windows onto redeeming love. Hosea pays to reclaim a wife who is already his but has given herself to another, and Christ pays with His blood to reclaim a people who had sold themselves to sin. You were bought at a price, Paul says, and here we see the price and the love behind it. The closing promise, that Israel will return and seek David their king and come trembling to His goodness, is fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David. He is the Redeemer who buys the bride and the King to whom the repentant come home.
study.god: The LORD's love in this chapter is astonishing because of its object: an unfaithful people who love their idols. He does not wait for Israel to become lovable; He commands love toward the fallen and redeems at cost. His discipline, the long stretch without king or sacrifice, is not abandonment but a severe mercy designed to bring His people to a genuine, trembling return. This is a God who pays to have His people back.

crossReferences.note: You are not your own; you were bought at a price, the redemption Hosea's purchase foreshadows.
God promises one shepherd, David My servant, to rule His reunited people, the hope of Hosea 3:5.
Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her, redeeming an unworthy bride.
A future turning of Israel to the Lord, echoing the promised return of Hosea 3:5.

study.oneStory: Hosea 3 concentrates the gospel into five verses: love that reaches the unlovely, redemption paid at real cost, and a people who will finally return to the LORD and their king. It points straight to Christ, who buys back His bride with His own blood and reigns as the Son of David to whom the repentant come home.

quiz.q: What made God's command to Hosea in verse 1 so striking?

options.text: He was to love and reclaim a wife who was unfaithful and now belonged to another, as a picture of God's love for Israel.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly links Hosea's love to the LORD's love for a people who turn to other gods.

options.text: He was told to find a new and better wife.
options.note: The point is reclaiming the same fallen wife, not replacing her.

options.text: He was to divorce her permanently.
options.note: The command is to love and restore, the opposite of divorce.

options.text: He was told to love her only if she changed first.
options.note: The love comes toward her while she is still unfaithful, not as a reward.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Hosea buying his wife back?

options.text: It pictures redemption, paying a price to reclaim someone who had become enslaved to unfaithfulness.
options.note: The price roughly equals that of a slave, foreshadowing being bought at a price.

options.text: It shows that love is mainly a financial transaction.
options.note: The payment dramatizes costly love, not a mercenary view of relationships.

options.text: It proves Gomer was worth the investment.
options.note: The point is grace toward the unworthy, not her worthiness.

options.text: It was an ordinary marriage custom with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The purchase is a deliberate sign of redemption.

quiz.q: What does the long period without king, sacrifice, or idol in verse 4 represent?

options.text: A season of stripping and waiting meant to bring Israel to a genuine return to God.
options.note: The deprivation parallels the terms Hosea set for Gomer, aimed at restoration.

options.text: God's final and permanent abandonment of Israel.
options.note: Verse 5 shows the waiting ends in return, not abandonment.

options.text: A time of great prosperity and freedom.
options.note: It is a time of loss and discipline, not prosperity.

options.text: Proof that worship no longer matters to God.
options.note: The aim is renewed true worship of the LORD.

quiz.q: Who is David their king in verse 5?

options.text: The promised Son of David, the Messiah fulfilled in Jesus, to whom the people return.
options.note: David was long dead; the prophecy looks to the Messianic king in David's line.

options.text: King David risen from the dead to rule again personally.
options.note: It points to David's greater Son, not David himself returning.

options.text: Any future political leader of Israel.
options.note: It specifically anticipates the Davidic Messiah, not just any ruler.

options.text: A symbol with no real fulfillment.
options.note: The New Testament shows it fulfilled in Christ, the Son of David.

quiz.q: What does this chapter reveal about God's love?

options.text: It is costly, freely given to the undeserving, and aimed at bringing His people home.
options.note: Redeeming love toward the unfaithful is the chapter's central revelation.

options.text: It is given only to those who earn it.
options.note: The love here reaches the unfaithful before they change, not as earned reward.

options.text: It is mostly words with no real action or cost.
options.note: The love is enacted at real cost, in silver and barley and commitment.

options.text: It eventually gives up on unfaithful people.
options.note: The chapter shows love that reclaims rather than abandons.

questions: God told Hosea to love his wife again while she was still unfaithful. How does it affect you to know that God loved you before you were lovely or willing?
Hosea paid a real price to reclaim his wife. What does it mean to you that Christ bought you back at the cost of His own blood?
Israel had to endure a long, stripped-down season before returning to God. Is there a barren stretch in your life that God may be using to draw you home?
The chapter promises a people who come trembling to the LORD and to His goodness. What would it look like for reverence and joy in God's goodness to grow together in you?
Repentance in Hosea is a returning, shuv. Is there an area where the Lord is inviting you to turn back to Him today?

translationNotes.note: An alternate rendering is Go show love to a woman who is loved by another; the flow of the marriage narrative favors reference to Gomer. The clause love raisin cakes reflects a literal Hebrew idiom for delighting in idol feasts.
Fifteen shekels of silver is about six ounces or 171 grams; a homer and a lethech of barley is roughly a homer and a half, together approximating the price of a slave.
David their king refers to the promised ruler in David's line, the Messiah, rather than David personally, as David had long since died.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 3:1-5. No em dashes required substitution in this passage.
